topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My hands, already clammy, began to shake as I reread the header: "Congratulations on your published work, [Wrong Name]." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward until my chest felt constricted, making each breath shallow and tight. I stared at the screen, the words blurring slightly. How could this have happened? I frantically clicked through the publication portal, searching for any trace of my submission, my name, anywhere near this article. Every search yielded nothing but the published piece, perfectly polished, bearing a name that wasn't mine. A low, involuntary groan escaped my lips. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and paced the small office, running a hand repeatedly through my already disheveled hair. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I sat hunched over my laptop, the glow of the screen reflecting dully in my wide, exhausted eyes. The notification popped up, and a wave of nausea washed over me. It was a confirmation—my piece was live, but under a completely different identity. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the ache in my molars. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and closed my eyes, trying to force a calm that refused to settle. When I opened them again, the reality hit with sickening force. I started typing, not to fix it, but to furiously draft an email, my fingers flying across the keys with jerky, uneven movements. Every sentence felt clumsy, inadequate. I kept glancing at the clock, the seconds stretching into agonizingly long intervals. A sharp intake of breath escaped me, followed by a muttered curse under my breath, a sound thin and brittle in the quiet apartment.

[story 3]

The crisp white paper felt alien in my hands. I had just received the final proof, and the name printed at the top was utterly foreign. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out all other thought. I traced the unfamiliar signature with a trembling finger, a prickling sensation crawling up my arm. Panic flared hot in my throat. I snatched my phone, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, and began dialing numbers, my voice tight and strained as I tried to speak to the editor. Every attempt to articulate what I was seeing dissolved into a rush of desperate, breathless apologies. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of books; the sudden noise made me flinch violently. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically block out the sight, my whole body rigid with contained turmoil.
